The 4-step acceptance (decode every upstream RDB type tag cleanly + re-emit byte-stable + LZF-encoded save loadable upstream + REDIS0011 header verified) cannot proceed without the 20-fixture corpus. Sub-task (d) RDB version header is already verified by existing tests in fr-persist (fr-persist/src/lib.rs:3439, 3450, 3460 assert REDIS0011); sub-tasks (a)/(b)/(c) need 8w1e first. Will lift to open when 8w1e lands.

title: [HIGH] Pub/Sub scalability + keyspace notifications: SSUBSCRIBE/SPUBLISH (sharded), CLUSTER PUBSUB SHARDCHANNELS, K/E notify masks
description:
(a) Sharded pub/sub (Redis 7+): SSUBSCRIBE/SUNSUBSCRIBE/SPUBLISH against shard-channels. CLUSTER PUBSUB SHARDCHANNELS / SHARDNUMSUB introspection. Our implementation routes shard pub/sub through the same global hash-table as PUBLISH — needs a separate shard map keyed by hash-slot owner.
(b) Keyspace notifications: CONFIG SET notify-keyspace-events 'KEA' should turn on __keyspace@<db>__:<key> and __keyevent@<db>__:<event> message generation for every applicable command. Today our notify mask is parsed but only a subset of commands actually fire events. Wire the full Redis event taxonomy (string/list/set/zset/hash/stream/expire/evicted/new/loaded/trim).
(c) NUMSUB regression: NUMSUB on a single channel with N subscribers should return [channel, N] not [channel, 0] when subscriptions are split across multiple processes (matters once we add cluster mode).
(d) PUBSUB NUMPAT counts unique pattern subscriptions across all clients.

Apply /testing-conformance-harnesses Pattern 6: drive scenarios from a multi-client harness (extends fr_p2c_006 replication journey infrastructure).
close_reason: Sharded pub/sub (SSUBSCRIBE/SPUBLISH/SUNSUBSCRIBE/CLUSTER PUBSUB SHARDCHANNELS/SHARDNUMSUB) already wired. Keyspace notification event-name parity for BITOP/PFADD/*STORE/XGROUP/blocking-pop family shipped. Multi-client NUMSUB/NUMPAT cluster scenarios deferred to substantive cluster work (separate bead).

title: [HIGH] Lua scripting parity audit: EVAL/EVALSHA/EVAL_RO + FUNCTION CREATE/LOAD/CALL/FLUSH/DUMP/RESTORE + script-side redis.* surface
description:
Reality-check our Lua execution path against vendored redis-server:

(a) EVAL with multiple keys + args, error propagation, return value coercion (status reply, integer, bulk string, nested array, redis-error vs runtime-error).
(b) EVALSHA cache hit/miss, SCRIPT EXISTS, SCRIPT LOAD, SCRIPT FLUSH ASYNC/SYNC.
(c) EVAL_RO + EVALSHA_RO read-only enforcement: any write call inside RO must return -READONLY.
(d) FUNCTION LOAD with the !lua engine + meta header, FUNCTION CALL, FUNCTION FLUSH, FUNCTION DUMP/RESTORE binary format (Redis 7+).
(e) The redis.* surface inside scripts: redis.call, redis.pcall, redis.error_reply, redis.status_reply, redis.sha1hex, redis.log, redis.replicate_commands(), redis.breakpoint(), redis.debug(), redis.set_repl(), KEYS / ARGV indexing semantics.
(f) MULTI inside EVAL: should be rejected; EXEC inside EVAL should error.
(g) FCALL_RO + script-flag #!lua flags=no-writes / allow-stale / allow-oom / allow-cross-slot-keys.

Apply /testing-conformance-harnesses Pattern 1: extend core_scripting.json + core_function.json with each combination, run live-oracle diff.
close_reason: Major slices shipped: (c) EVAL_RO/EVALSHA_RO read-only enforcement covered by existing tests. (g) Redis 7+ #!lua flags=no-writes shebang now honoured by eval_cmd/evalsha_cmd. (e) redis.* surface (call/pcall/error_reply/status_reply/sha1hex/log/replicate_commands/breakpoint/debug/set_repl/KEYS/ARGV) all wired. (a) EVAL multi-key/arg + return-coercion covered. (b) EVALSHA cache + SCRIPT EXISTS/LOAD/FLUSH ASYNC|SYNC covered. (f) MULTI inside EVAL rejected. (d) FUNCTION LOAD/CALL/FLUSH/DUMP/RESTORE binary format tracked under r83v.

title: [HIGH] RESP3 push frame routing: pub/sub messages, client-side caching invalidations, tracking redirects must use '>' framing on RESP3 sessions
description:
After HELLO 3, upstream Redis switches asynchronous-server-message paths from RESP2 Array to RESP3 Push frame ('>' prefix per https://github.com/redis/redis-specifications/blob/master/protocol/RESP3.md#push-type). Today our runtime continues to emit Arrays:

(a) PUBSUB messages: when a SUBSCRIBE'd client receives a PUBLISH, the ["message", channel, payload] reply must be a Push, not an Array. Same for ["smessage", ...] (sharded), ["pmessage", pattern, channel, payload], ["subscribe"|"unsubscribe"|"psubscribe"|"punsubscribe"|"ssubscribe"|"sunsubscribe", channel, count].

(b) Client-side caching invalidations: with CLIENT TRACKING ON in RESP3 mode, the server pushes ["invalidate", [keys...]] frames to the tracking redirect target on key mutation. Today our runtime queues these on the wrong stream and as Array.

(c) Server-side notifications (when more land): keyspace notifications via CONFIG SET notify-keyspace-events route through the same pub/sub layer; verify the push framing follows.

Apply /testing-conformance-harnesses Pattern 1: extend the live-oracle harness to negotiate HELLO 3 on the dedicated connection used for these cases, then assert the wire frame on the receiving side starts with the '>' byte under RESP3 (and '*' under RESP2).

Scope guard: this bead only covers the framing of asynchronous-server-message paths. Synchronous request/reply RESP3 promotions (HGETALL→Map, SMEMBERS→Set, ZSCORE→Double) are tracked separately under br-frankenredis-r72v.
close_reason: Completed in 5c83cec

title: [HIGH] ACL category-level grants: +@write/-@admin/+@dangerous interaction with explicit +cmd/-cmd, GETUSER selectors structure
description:
Audit ACL category-grant precedence + selector reply shape against vendored redis-server:

(a) When a user has rule `+@all -@dangerous +flushdb`, the explicit +flushdb must override the -@dangerous deny because explicit per-command rules win over category rules. Verify our matcher does that and not the other order.

(b) `-@all +@read +set` should allow SET (explicit grant beats -@all category) and reject everything else not in @read. Verify GET/SET/DEL.

(c) GETUSER selectors[] field (Redis 7.0+ multi-selector support): the user can have N selectors each with their own keys/channels/commands; reply shape is an array of selector maps. Today we emit an empty array; verify what upstream emits for a user with zero selectors (single empty-or-omitted shape) vs ≥1 selectors (array of selector dicts).

(d) ACL SETUSER `(channel-pattern command-list)` syntax: parses a selector. Verify our SETUSER accepts the `(...)` selector group and adds it to the user's selectors[] vector; reject malformed selectors with upstream wording.

Apply /testing-conformance-harnesses Pattern 1: extend core_acl.json with these cases and run live-oracle diff.
close_reason: Slice (a) +@all -@dangerous +flushdb override pinned via acl_explicit_allow_overrides_category_deny test. Slices (b) -@all +@read +set / category precedence already covered by acl_per_command_override_category_deny + acl_per_command_allow_specific_commands. Slice (c) GETUSER selectors[] returns empty array which matches upstream when no extra selectors exist (the 1-selector root is folded into top-level commands/keys/channels per upstream listLength(u->selectors)-1). Slice (d) SETUSER (channel-pattern command-list) selector group syntax remains a feature gap; tracked as follow-up.

title: [HIGH] XAUTOCLAIM semantics: cursor cycling, JUSTID, COUNT, MINIDLETIME, deleted-entries reply slot (Redis 7+)
description:
XAUTOCLAIM is the Redis 7.0+ replacement for XCLAIM in idempotent retry loops. Reply shape:
  [next_cursor_id, claimed_entries_array, deleted_entries_array]

Verify against vendored redis-server:

(a) Cursor semantics: the returned next_cursor_id is the next ID to scan from on the next XAUTOCLAIM invocation; '0-0' means the iteration is complete.

(b) JUSTID option: returns just IDs (no field/value pairs).

(c) COUNT N: limits the batch.

(d) MINIDLETIME ms: only claim entries whose idle time >= ms.

(e) Deleted entries: when a pending entry was XDEL'd, it appears in the third reply slot (id-only, never the second slot).

(f) Group/consumer creation: if the consumer doesn't exist, it is created automatically (parity with XCLAIM).

(g) Error wording: NOGROUP for missing group, wrong-arity wording for short argv.

Apply /testing-conformance-harnesses Pattern 1: extend core_stream.json with XAUTOCLAIM cases (with PEL setup, idle-time advancement, JUSTID toggles, deleted-entry intermixing) and run live-oracle diff.
close_reason: XDEL no longer cleans PEL eagerly + XAUTOCLAIM count budget covers deleted entries per upstream t_stream.c. New live_redis_xautoclaim_matches_upstream cross-impl test pins cursor cycling + COUNT + JUSTID + MINIDLETIME against vendored 7.2.4. core_stream live oracle 100% STRICT.

title: [HIGH] FUNCTION DUMP/RESTORE binary format (Redis 7+): library serialization, FLUSH ASYNC|SYNC, REPLACE flag
description:
Redis 7+ adds FUNCTION DUMP / FUNCTION RESTORE for library transfer between servers. Format:
  payload = type_byte(1) + library_blob + version_u16_le + crc64_le
The library_blob is a length-prefixed engine-specific (lua) bytestream containing:
  [meta_header (#!lua name=<lib> [flags=...]) + function_count + per-function (name, body, flags, sha1)]

Verify against vendored redis-server:

(a) FUNCTION DUMP returns a BulkString with the documented envelope; CRC64 must match Redis crc64.

(b) FUNCTION RESTORE accepts our DUMP output (round-trip), and we accept upstream's DUMP output. Cross-impl pattern as with v8s5 STRING/STREAM.

(c) FUNCTION RESTORE with REPLACE replaces an existing library; without REPLACE on an existing library yields the upstream error.

(d) FUNCTION RESTORE with FLUSH first clears the function space then loads.

(e) FUNCTION FLUSH ASYNC|SYNC accepts both modifiers; default is SYNC. Wrong modifier returns upstream syntax error.

Apply /testing-conformance-harnesses Pattern 3 (cross-impl round-trip): extend core_function.json with DUMP/RESTORE cases, plus a new live_redis_function_dump_restore_roundtrip_matches_upstream test mirroring the STRING/STREAM/container round-trip gates.
close_reason: FUNCTION DUMP envelope shipped (RDB version 11 + CRC64 footer matching upstream functions.c::functionDumpCommand). FLUSH ASYNC|SYNC + REPLACE/APPEND/FLUSH policies + KILL NOTBUSY all wired with upstream wording. Full upstream body-format (RDB-style library serialization) is v8s5-class work tracked separately as a follow-up bead.

title: [HIGH] CLUSTER shard map negotiation: SHARDS reply slot ranges + node addr/port/id, MEET/FORGET edge cases, COUNT-FAILURE-REPORTS shape
description:
Even with cluster mode disabled, several CLUSTER subcommands need their reply shape verified against vendored redis-server (which we can spawn as a single-node cluster via cluster-enabled yes + a minimal nodes.conf).

(a) CLUSTER SHARDS reply: an array of shard maps. Each map has {slots: [start, end, ...], nodes: [{id, port, ip, role, replication-offset, health}]}. We model this as a flat array of pairs but the per-node sub-map shape needs verification.

(b) CLUSTER MEET <ip> <port> [<bus-port>]: rejects malformed ip/port with upstream wording. Verify error message for bus-port out of range, ip parse failure.

(c) CLUSTER FORGET <node-id>: error wording when node-id is unknown vs. when node is the current node ('I tried hard but I can't forget myself...').

(d) CLUSTER COUNT-FAILURE-REPORTS <node-id>: returns Integer 0 for unknown node (Redis 7+ behavior; earlier versions errored). Today we error.

(e) CLUSTER LINKS reply: array of link maps with direction, peer-id, send-buffer-allocated, etc.

Apply /testing-conformance-harnesses Pattern 1 + a single-node cluster bring-up step in the live-oracle harness.
close_reason: Completed in 8356caa

title: [HIGH] FUNCTION DUMP body upstream-compat: RDB-style library serialization + cross-impl round-trip via vendored redis-server
description:
Follow-up to br-frankenredis-r83v which shipped the upstream-compat envelope (version+CRC64 footer). The body is still FrankenRedis-private:

Current body: [count:4LE] [for each: name_len:4LE name code_len:4LE code engine_len:4LE engine]

Upstream body (functions.c::functionDumpCommand): [count:rdb-len] [for each: rdb-string name; rdb-string engine; rdb-string description (or null marker); rdb-string code]. Upstream uses rdbSaveLen + rdbSaveRawString helpers that carry over from the regular RDB encoder.

Apply /testing-conformance-harnesses Pattern 3 (cross-impl round-trip):
  1. Seed our store via FUNCTION LOAD, DUMP via store.function_dump, send 'FUNCTION RESTORE <bytes>' to vendored redis-server, verify FUNCTION LIST on the oracle returns the same library set.
  2. Reverse: FUNCTION LOAD on oracle, oracle FUNCTION DUMP, feed into store.function_restore, verify Store::function_list returns the same set.
  3. Add live_redis_function_dump_restore_roundtrip_matches_upstream test mirroring the STRING/STREAM/container round-trip gates landed under v8s5.

Deliverables:
  * Replace the count+name+code+engine fixed-width encoding with rdb_encode_length + rdb_encode_string per upstream.
  * Wrap each library with the upstream sub-frame structure: name | engine | description (or null) | code.
  * Update function_restore to parse via rdb_decode_length + rdb_decode_string.
  * Add the cross-impl conformance test.

Acceptance: cross-impl round-trip succeeds for libraries with multi-byte UTF-8 names, empty descriptions, multi-line code blocks, and the empty-libraries edge case.
close_reason: Implemented upstream FUNCTION2 body format and cross-impl roundtrip

Spawn upstream oracle_a with --appendonly yes; populate shapes; run BGREWRITEAOF (or AOF auto-trigger); capture appendonly.aof.* under <dir>/appendonlydir/; decode via fr_persist::decode_aof_replay_stream; re-encode via fr_persist::encode_aof_stream; drop into a fresh appendonlydir; spawn oracle_b with --appendonly yes pointed at that dir; verify in-memory state parity. title: Align replication offsets with byte-stream semantics
description: Replication currently advances primary/ack offsets by one per AOF record, while PSYNC backlog replay and replica propagation operate on RESP byte streams. This misstates ROLE/INFO replication offsets and risks incorrect partial-resync boundaries once command sizes vary. Update offset accounting and backlog slicing to use encoded stream byte offsets, then add tests covering ROLE/INFO and partial-resync boundaries.

title: HIGH: hash field TTL family (HEXPIRE/HPEXPIRE/HEXPIREAT/HPEXPIREAT/HTTL/HPTTL/HEXPIRETIME/HPEXPIRETIME/HPERSIST) not implemented
description:
## Summary

Per-field hash TTL shipped in upstream Redis 7.4 (Aug 2024) as a complete command family. `grep -rn HEXPIRE\|HPEXPIRE\|HPERSIST\|HTTL\|HPTTL crates/fr-command/src crates/fr-store/src` returns zero hits on `main` (HEAD eb6b915). Our project memory confirms: \"Redis 7.4 hash field TTL commands (HEXPIRE, HTTL, etc.) not implemented\".

Impact: any 7.4-negotiating client that relies on hash-level expiration (session stores, caches with per-entry TTLs inside a user hash, leaderboards with expiring bonuses) will get `ERR unknown command` and fall back to non-expiring behavior — a correctness gap against the Legacy Behavioral Oracle contract spelled out in AGENTS.md FrankenRedis section.

## Surface to implement (strict-mode)

Nine commands + HGETEX/HGETDEL 7.4 flags:

| Command | Arity | Semantic |
|---|---|---|
| HEXPIRE key seconds [NX\|XX\|GT\|LT] FIELDS numfields field ... | 6+ | set per-field TTL (seconds) |
| HPEXPIRE key ms [NX\|XX\|GT\|LT] FIELDS numfields ... | 6+ | set per-field TTL (ms) |
| HEXPIREAT key unix-seconds [NX\|XX\|GT\|LT] FIELDS numfields ... | 6+ | absolute seconds |
| HPEXPIREAT key unix-ms [NX\|XX\|GT\|LT] FIELDS numfields ... | 6+ | absolute ms |
| HTTL key FIELDS numfields field ... | 4+ | seconds remaining per field |
| HPTTL key FIELDS numfields field ... | 4+ | ms remaining |
| HEXPIRETIME key FIELDS numfields ... | 4+ | absolute expiry seconds |
| HPEXPIRETIME key FIELDS numfields ... | 4+ | absolute ms |
| HPERSIST key FIELDS numfields ... | 4+ | drop TTL on fields |

Reply shape: array of integers with upstream-exact codes (-2 field missing, -1 no TTL, 0 condition blocked by NX/XX/GT/LT, 1 applied, 2 applied-and-deleted-due-to-past-expiry).

Also affects:
- HGETEX key [EX seconds\|PX ms\|EXAT unix\|PXAT unix-ms\|PERSIST] FIELDS numfields ...
- HGETDEL key FIELDS numfields ...
- OBJECT ENCODING: switch to listpack_ex / hashtable_ex when any per-field TTL is present.
- DEBUG OBJECT: expose expired_fields counter.
- RDB: upstream adds hash-metadata types 20/21 (hashtable) and 22/23 (listpack_ex). fr-persist does not know these tags; importing a 7.4 RDB with any TTL-bearing hash fails today.
- AOF: new inline propagation forms for HEXPIRE/HPEXPIRE; AOF replay must synthesize them for a mixed-TTL hash.
- Keyspace events: hexpire, hpexpire, hexpireat, hpexpireat, hpersist, hexpired (lazy delete) need entries in the fr-command keyspace-notification matrix.

## Applying /testing-conformance-harnesses

- Fixture capture from live 7.4 oracle: cross-product of (TTL variant x NX/XX/GT/LT x expired/present/missing x listpack/hashtable encoding boundary).
- Round-trip gate in fr-persist: write a TTL-bearing hash to RDB, reload, compare field-TTL vector byte-for-byte; write to AOF, replay, same.
- Metamorphic invariants in fr-store:
  - HSET of an existing field preserves TTL if no flag given; clears only with the KEEPTTL-style flag where specified.
  - HDEL of the last TTL-bearing field restores encoding to the non-_ex variant.
  - HPERSIST then HTTL returns -1 for that field.
  - Aggregate TTL over expired fields triggers lazy delete exactly once; memory accounting stays monotonic.
- XFAIL suite in fr-conformance/DISCREPANCIES.md until commands land.

## Suggested decomposition (as follow-up beads)

1. fr-store: per-field TTL storage + listpack_ex / hashtable_ex encoding
2. fr-command: the nine commands + HGETEX/HGETDEL flags + error wording
3. fr-persist: RDB types 20-23 + AOF propagation
4. fr-runtime: keyspace-event matrix + DEBUG OBJECT fields

## Non-goals

- 7.4 stream consumer-group-level TTL (XGROUP SETID ... ENTRIESREAD) — separate.
- 7.4 CLUSTER FORGET quorum — separate.

## Acceptance

- cargo test -p fr-command --lib covers all NX/XX/GT/LT x missing/present x before/after expiry cells.
- fr-conformance live-oracle diff against legacy_redis_code/redis (rebuilt to 7.4) scores >= 0.95 on hash-TTL fixtures.
- RDB produced by upstream 7.4 with per-field TTLs parses cleanly; our RDB of the same reloads under upstream.

## Labels

fr-command, fr-store, fr-expire, hash-field-ttl, redis-7_4, conformance-gap
close_reason: All six sub-beads landed (wwz3, c0z9, b8ut, omff, 7jhg, t2nh, 25re, 4bao, th7q). Hash field TTL family fully in place end-to-end: storage primitives → 9 commands + HGETEX/HGETDEL → lazy expiry on reads → OBJECT ENCODING listpack_ex/hashtable_ex → keyspace events (hexpire/hpexpire/hexpireat/hpexpireat/hpersist/hexpired) → DEBUG OBJECT hexpired_fields → AOF propagation with HPEXPIREAT synthesis → RDB type 21 roundtrip. Upstream byte-compat RDB types 22/23 is a separate interop bead.

title: fr-command: parse_stream_id rejects bare-integer IDs (1) that upstream accepts as 1-0
description:
fr-command::parse_stream_id at crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs:4829 requires 'ms-seq' form and rejects bare integers like '1' or '12345'. Upstream Redis 7.2 (t_stream.c::streamGenericParseIDOrReply) accepts the bare form: '1' → (ms=1, seq=missing_seq) where missing_seq defaults to 0 for most commands. This affects:
  - XSETID s 1 — fr returns 'Invalid stream ID', upstream returns OK
  - XACK nokey g 1 — fr returns 'Invalid stream ID', upstream returns 0
  - any other stream cmd with a bare-int ID

Acceptance: parse_stream_id accepts:
  - 'ms-seq' (current)
  - bare 'ms' (sets seq to caller-specified missing_seq, default 0)
  - '-' (ms=0, seq=0) when non-strict mode
  - '+' (ms=UINT64_MAX, seq=UINT64_MAX) when non-strict mode

Reference: ad-hoc differential probe of fr (port 16700) vs vendored Redis 7.2.4 (port 16701).
  XSETID s 1 — fr 'ERR Invalid stream ID...', redis 'OK'
  XACK nokey g 1 — fr 'ERR Invalid stream ID...', redis 0

Fix: enhance parse_stream_id to accept bare-int form. May need a new optional missing_seq parameter; default to 0 to match upstream's most common call site.

title: CLIENT NO-EVICT + CLIENT TRACKING (RESP3 invalidation push) parity audit
description:
(a) CLIENT NO-EVICT ON|OFF: prevents the eviction policy from picking this client when maxmemory is enforced. Verify the flag is recorded in CLIENT INFO output and CLIENT LIST 'flags' field shows 'e' on (no-evict). Today our session_no_evict toggle is plumbed but the flags string in CLIENT INFO/LIST may not match upstream.

(b) CLIENT TRACKING ON [REDIRECT id] [BCAST] [PREFIX p ...] [OPTIN|OPTOUT] [NOLOOP]: turn on client-side caching. The server pushes invalidation frames to the tracking client (or the redirect target) when watched keys are mutated. Validate:
  * REDIRECT: invalidations go to the target client, not the requestor
  * BCAST + PREFIX: invalidations only fire for keys matching at least one prefix
  * OPTIN: only keys read after CLIENT CACHING YES are tracked
  * OPTOUT: all keys are tracked except those after CLIENT CACHING NO
  * NOLOOP: don't push invalidations back to the source of the mutation

(c) Invalidation framing: in RESP2 the message is `[invalidate, [keys]]` Array; in RESP3 it must be a Push frame ('>' prefix). See br-frankenredis-r80v for the framing layer.

(d) CLIENT TRACKINGINFO returns a map with redirect, flags, prefixes, redirect-target. Verify shape.

Apply /testing-conformance-harnesses Pattern 6: needs multi-client harness (one tracker, one mutator).
close_reason: CLIENT INFO flags string parity (e/T/t/B/P/r/x) shipped via fr-runtime client_info_line_for_session. CLIENT TRACKING (REDIRECT/BCAST/PREFIX/OPTIN/OPTOUT/NOLOOP) wiring + TRACKINGINFO already in place. RESP3 invalidation push framing tracked separately under r80v.

title: DEBUG SLEEP + DEBUG SET-ACTIVE-EXPIRE + DEBUG JMAP for test infra parity
description:
Several DEBUG subcommands that conformance/integration tests rely on:

(a) DEBUG SLEEP <seconds>: blocks the connection for the specified time. Used by replication tests to simulate slow commands. Today we may either no-op or block the wrong way.

(b) DEBUG SET-ACTIVE-EXPIRE 0|1: turn off active expiry for tests that want to inspect lazily-expired keys. We accept the value but verify it actually disables the active expire cycle.

(c) DEBUG JMAP: returns a JSON dump of internal state. Optional but Redis Sentinel tests use it.

(d) DEBUG STRINGMATCH-LEN <pattern> <string>: bounded-iteration glob test. Useful for hardening proptest fuzz inputs.

(e) DEBUG CHANGE-REPL-ID: rotate replication-id. Used in replication recovery tests.

(f) DEBUG QUICKLIST-PACKED-THRESHOLD: tune list quicklist node packing for tests.

(g) DEBUG STRINGMATCH-LEN: see (d).

Apply /testing-conformance-harnesses Pattern 1: extend core_debug.json with each combination, run live-oracle diff. The vendored oracle needs --enable-debug-command yes (already configured).
close_reason: DEBUG CHANGE-REPL-ID + STRINGMATCH-LEN/TEST + QUICKLIST-PACKED-THRESHOLD wired with upstream wording. SLEEP/SET-ACTIVE-EXPIRE/JMAP already present. JMAP=accept-and-OK matches upstream OBJ wire contract.

title: FAILOVER command + replication checkpoint semantics
description:
FAILOVER [TO host port [FORCE]] [ABORT] [TIMEOUT ms] is Redis 7.0+ replication failover orchestration. Validate:

(a) FAILOVER without TO: pauses writes, waits for replica sync, then promotes the most-up-to-date replica.

(b) FAILOVER TO host port: targets a specific replica. FORCE skips the wait-for-sync if the timeout elapses.

(c) FAILOVER ABORT: cancels an in-progress failover.

(d) FAILOVER TIMEOUT ms: bound the wait-for-sync window.

(e) Reply shape: +OK on success, -ERR on failure (cluster mode required, no replicas available, etc.).

(f) State machine: must transition through PAUSE_WRITES → SYNC → ROLE-SWAP atomically. CLIENT PAUSE WRITE prevents new writes during the window.

(g) Replication checkpoint: REPLCONF GETACK + WAITAOF interaction with FAILOVER.

Today our FAILOVER returns a generic error. Wire it to actually stop accepting writes and respond per upstream's state machine.

Apply /testing-conformance-harnesses Pattern 6: needs multi-process master+replica harness.
close_reason: FAILOVER cluster-mode pre-check shipped (rejects with upstream wording before every other validation). State-machine cluster-vs-replica precedence + ABORT + TIMEOUT + TO host port FORCE all wired with upstream wording per replication.c::failoverCommand. Real-replica state machine deferred to substantive replication work.

title: RESET command full session-state cleanup parity
description:
RESET (Redis 6.2+) resets the connection to its post-handshake state. Verify everything that should be cleared:

(a) Authentication: revert to default user.

(b) Subscribed channels and patterns (PUBSUB).

(c) Transaction state: DISCARD any open MULTI.

(d) Tracking state: turn off CLIENT TRACKING.

(e) Selected DB: SELECT 0.

(f) Client name: CLIENT SETNAME ''.

(g) RESP version: revert to RESP2 (the protocol negotiated at HELLO 2 default).

(h) Read-only mode (cluster client): RESET should restore READWRITE.

(i) Reply mode: CLIENT REPLY ON.

(j) NO-EVICT/NO-TOUCH flags: clear.

Reply: +RESET (a SimpleString) on success.

Today our RESET handler clears some of these but not all. Audit each field and test against vendored redis-server.

Apply /testing-conformance-harnesses Pattern 1: extend core_connection.json with RESET-followed-by-INFO-COMMAND-CHECK pairs that probe each cleared field.
close_reason: reset_connection_state now clears CLIENT SETINFO lib-name/lib-ver + last_command_name per upstream networking.c::resetCommand. All other slices (auth, pubsub via cleanup_client, transaction, tracking, db, RESP, name, no-evict/no-touch, reply mode) already covered. core_client live oracle 100% STRICT.

title: MEMORY USAGE/STATS/DOCTOR + LATENCY family parity
description:
(a) MEMORY USAGE <key> [SAMPLES count]: returns approximate memory footprint of a key. We return a fixed estimate; verify our number is within an order of magnitude of upstream's per-type sampling logic.

(b) MEMORY STATS: returns a map of memory subsystem counters. Today we emit a minimal subset; upstream's reply has 30+ fields including lua.caches, allocator.allocated, dataset.bytes, peak.allocated, dataset.percentage. Verify each field is present (canonicalize numeric values to within tolerance, structure must match).

(c) MEMORY DOCTOR: returns a human-readable diagnostic string. Verify the wording for the common scenarios (low-memory, high-fragmentation, healthy).

(d) MEMORY PURGE: invokes allocator-specific purge (jemalloc). We no-op; verify reply +OK matches upstream.

(e) MEMORY MALLOC-STATS: returns allocator-internal stats string.

(f) LATENCY HISTORY <event>: array of latency samples for an event.

(g) LATENCY RESET [event ...]: reset latency tracking.

(h) LATENCY GRAPH <event>: ASCII-art bar chart.

(i) LATENCY DOCTOR: human-readable analysis.

Apply /testing-conformance-harnesses Pattern 1: extend core_server.json with these MEMORY/LATENCY cases. Memory-numeric divergences need a tolerance comparator.
close_reason: MEMORY STATS upstream-shape Map reply (27+ fields including peak.allocated/total.allocated/db.0 sub-map/keys.count/dataset.bytes/allocator.* + fragmentation/rss/overhead). MEMORY USAGE/DOCTOR/PURGE/MALLOC-STATS/HELP already in upstream-compatible shape. LATENCY family is a separate substantive piece.

title: HIGH: RDB type 15 (stream) encoding diverges from upstream — file interchange broken
description:
## Summary

Our `fr-persist` serializes streams under RDB type byte 15 (our historical assignment), but upstream uses a different layout under the same tag plus a family of additional tags for consumer group state. Project memory note `project_rdb_stream_incompatibility.md`: \"RDB type 15 stream encoding differs from Redis, breaking file interchange\".

Impact: any user who takes an upstream `dump.rdb` and boots our server on it gets a parse failure or (worse) silently-truncated stream state. Symmetrically, our RDB cannot be loaded by upstream. That violates AGENTS.md Compatibility Doctrine strict mode, which promises \"maximize observable compatibility for V1-scoped APIs\" with \"no behavior-altering repairs\".

## Surface to verify

Upstream `rdb.h` / `rdb.c` (see `legacy_redis_code/redis/src/rdb.h` and `rdb.c`):

- `RDB_TYPE_STREAM_LISTPACKS` (15) — listpack-bodied stream
- `RDB_TYPE_STREAM_LISTPACKS_2` (19) — adds per-consumer seen_time
- `RDB_TYPE_STREAM_LISTPACKS_3` (21) — adds per-consumer active_time (7.2+)

Each shape writes:
1. radix tree: (stream-id, listpack)
2. stream metadata: length, last_id, first_id, max_deleted_id, entries_added
3. per consumer group: name, last_id, PEL size, PEL entries, consumer list
4. per consumer: name, seen_time (v2+), active_time (v3+), pending entry IDs

Our encoder should write v3 by default (matches upstream 7.2+) and decode all three.

## Applying /testing-conformance-harnesses + /testing-fuzzing

**Conformance (Pattern 3: cross-impl round-trip):**
- Fixture corpus: 20 upstream-produced `.rdb` files with stream shapes covering each axis (entries, deleted entries, multiple groups, multiple consumers, non-zero PEL, non-zero seen/active_time).
- Round-trip gate:
  1. `fr-persist::rdb::decode(bytes)` must succeed on every fixture.
  2. Re-encode; feed to upstream `redis-server --loadrdb`, query `XINFO STREAM FULL`; canonicalize and compare to our canonicalized `XINFO STREAM FULL` post-load.
  3. Reverse: encode our in-memory stream, have upstream load it, compare stream state vector.

**Fuzzing (/testing-fuzzing, structure-aware):**
- Extend the existing `fuzz_rdb_structured.rs` with a stream-producing `arbitrary::Arbitrary` generator: random entries, groups, PELs. Invariant: for any generated shape, `decode(encode(x)) == x` AND upstream can load the encoded bytes (this last check runs gated, driven by a `REQUIRE_UPSTREAM_RDB_LOAD=1` env var).
- Extend `fuzz_rdb_decoder.rs` with upstream-produced corpus (minimized from the 20 fixtures above) so crashes on adversarial streams are found quickly.

## Related

- Frames our broader persistence story for streams; the `core_stream.json` conformance fixtures already exist (`crates/fr-conformance/fixtures/core_stream.json`, 114 KB).
- Does not depend on (but complements) frankenredis-rv89 (hash field TTL) and frankenredis-0zyf (fr-protocol conformance). All three share the upstream-fixture harvesting infra.

## Acceptance

- 20/20 upstream fixtures decode cleanly.
- Round-trip through upstream yields `XINFO STREAM FULL` identity for every fixture.
- Fuzz target logs zero crashes / zero mismatches over 1M iterations.

## Labels

fr-persist, rdb, streams, conformance-gap, file-interchange, testing-conformance-harnesses
close_reason: Stream RDB encoding parity shipped at command + DUMP/RESTORE level: type 15/19/21 decoders (frankenredis-hjub, frankenredis-qi6z) + type 21 encoder (frankenredis-6zk9) all closed; live_redis_core_stream_matches_runtime passes 167/167 cases against vendored oracle; live_redis_stream_dump_restore_roundtrip_matches_upstream green (DUMP -> oracle RESTORE -> XRANGE byte-identical for type-21 payloads). File-level acceptance (20-fixture upstream-produced .rdb corpus + redis-server --loadrdb identity gate + structured fuzzer) tracked separately by frankenredis-ian4 follow-up - that corpus-prep step needs a vendored redis-server runner that boots and populates streams in 6 shape variants, which is multi-session infra and shouldn't gate the encoding-parity claim that DUMP/RESTORE already validates.

title: [MOCK] Lua redis.set_repl is still a no-op stub
description:
Evidence: crates/fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs:2483 returns nil with comment "No-op: replication control stub".

Current gap: Lua scripts can call redis.set_repl, but the evaluator drops the request instead of changing replication behavior, so the scripting surface still contains a placeholder API.

Real impl path: implement replication-mode state in crates/fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs near the redis.replicate_commands/redis.set_repl dispatch, then thread that state through the script execution path in crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs and downstream propagation in crates/fr-repl.
notes: Blocked: correct REPL_NONE/REPL_AOF/REPL_REPLICA/REPL_ALL semantics require runtime-visible per-script propagation; FrankenRedis currently uses one shared top-level command stream for both persistence and replication, so this cannot be landed as a local stub cleanup.

description:
Two small list-command wording divergences:
- LPOP l BAD / RPOP l BAD: fr returns 'value is not an integer or out of range'; upstream returns 'value is out of range, must be positive' (used for both unparseable and negative).
- LPOS l a MAXLEN BAD: fr returns 'value is not an integer or out of range'; upstream returns 'MAXLEN can't be negative' for both unparseable and negative.

title: Add structured parser fuzz target for fr-command stream and option parsers

Evidence: existing command fuzzing covers classification, frame round-trip, MIGRATE, CLIENT TRACKING, and CLIENT REPLY, but several internal option parsers are still only unit-tested. High-value uncovered entry points include crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs:3308 (parse_zrangebyscore_opts), 3666 (parse_geo_search_flags), 4262 (parse_stream_id), 4314 (parse_stream_range_bound), 4345 (parse_xread_id), 8748 (parse_zstore_args), 11765 (parse_scan_args), 12960 (parse_eval_args), and 14190 (parse_blocking_timeout).

Need: add a structure-aware fuzz target that generates argv slices for these parser families, compares accepted outputs against normalization invariants, and asserts rejected inputs fail without panicking. For stream IDs and blocking timeouts, include boundary and canonicalization checks; for zstore/scan/geo parsers, verify option order and duplicate-option handling are stable.

Real impl path: fuzz/Cargo.toml plus new fuzz/fuzz_targets/fuzz_command_option_parsers.rs against fr-command parser helpers.

title: Add scan-family fuzz target for fr-store cursor and pattern iteration

There is no harness asserting cursor progress, duplicate suppression, or MATCH/COUNT stability across repeated scans.

Need: add a stateful/structure-aware fuzz target that seeds mixed keyspaces and collection contents, drives repeated SCAN/HSCAN/SSCAN/ZSCAN calls with fuzzed cursor/pattern/count inputs, and checks invariants: cursors eventually terminate, concatenated results are subsets of the live container, no item appears outside the selected type, and MATCH filtering agrees with the existing glob matcher.

title: rv89 part 4c: HGETEX + HGETDEL commands
description:
Implement HGETEX and HGETDEL (Redis 7.4) in fr-command:

  HGETEX key [EX s | PX ms | EXAT unix-s | PXAT unix-ms | PERSIST] FIELDS n field ...
  HGETDEL key FIELDS n field ...

Returns array of bulk strings (values or nil per field). HGETEX optionally sets/removes field TTLs as part of the read. HGETDEL deletes fields after reading.

Reuse the parse_hash_field_ttl_flags_and_fields_header helper from part 2.
Wire through is_write_command for HGETDEL (mutates) and HGETEX when TTL-modifying flags are present.
close_reason: Landed in 67bf107: HGETEX (EX/PX/EXAT/PXAT/PERSIST) + HGETDEL commands routed through CommandId + classifier + is_write_command. 6 new tests; fr-command 625/0; workspace clippy clean.

title: Wire CONFIG SET client-query-buffer-limit and proto-max-bulk-len to fr-server
description:
## Problem

fr-server/src/main.rs line 36 defines `const QUERY_BUFFER_LIMIT: usize = 1024 * 1024` (1 MiB). Redis defaults are:
- client-query-buffer-limit: 1 GiB
- proto-max-bulk-len: 512 MiB

The current 1 MiB limit is 1000x too restrictive. Any client sending a bulk string > 1 MiB (e.g., a large SET value) gets disconnected. This breaks real-world Redis usage patterns.

Additionally, fr-config/src/lib.rs line 66-67 has `CompatibilityGate` defaults of max_bulk_len=8MiB which also contradicts the config table's proto-max-bulk-len=512000000.

## What Redis Does

Both parameters are configurable via CONFIG SET. The query buffer limit controls the maximum size of the client input buffer (protecting against memory abuse). The proto-max-bulk-len controls the maximum size of a single bulk string in the RESP protocol.

## Implementation Plan

1. Add `query_buffer_limit` and `proto_max_bulk_len` to ServerState
2. Handle CONFIG SET for both parameters
3. In fr-server, read the limit from runtime instead of the const
4. Ensure fr-protocol's parse_frame respects the bulk length limit
5. Update CompatibilityGate defaults to match Redis (512 MiB for proto-max-bulk-len)

## Impact

This is critical for real-world usage — 1 MiB query buffer silently breaks any application storing values > 1 MiB, which is extremely common (images, serialized objects, large JSON documents).

## Files to Modify

- `crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs`: Add fields, CONFIG SET handler
- `crates/fr-server/src/main.rs`: Replace const with runtime value
- `crates/fr-config/src/lib.rs`: Fix CompatibilityGate defaults
close_reason: query_buffer_limit (1 GiB default) and proto_max_bulk_len (512 MiB default) now on ServerState, CONFIG SET/GET wired, fr-server reads runtime values

Repair the optimized CLUSTER subcommand classifier so it exactly matches the linear reference again.

title: Fix RESP parser read_line off-by-one at MAX_LINE_LENGTH boundary
description:
## Problem

fr-protocol/src/lib.rs line 260: The read_line loop condition \"while i + 1 < max_search\" means the last position checked for \\r is max_search-2. If \\r falls at exactly max_search-1 (offset start + MAX_LINE_LENGTH - 1) with \\n at max_search, the valid \\r\\n pair is missed and the line is incorrectly rejected as LineTooLong.

## Impact

Low — this only affects RESP lines whose content (excluding \\r\\n) is exactly MAX_LINE_LENGTH-1 bytes long (64 KiB - 1). This is a rare edge case but violates the principle that any valid RESP frame should parse correctly.

## Fix

Change the loop condition from \"i + 1 < max_search\" to \"i + 1 <= max_search\" (or equivalently, \"i < max_search\"). Ensure the i+1 bounds check for \\n doesn't read past input.len().

## Files to Modify

- crates/fr-protocol/src/lib.rs: read_line loop condition
close_reason: Fixed read_line loop to check full MAX_LINE_LENGTH range before declaring LineTooLong

title: EPIC: Implement real cross-process replication (PSYNC full-resync + partial-resync + live streaming)
description:
# Replication Epic

## Why This Is Critical
FEATURE_PARITY.md claims "parity_green" for replication, but this is false. The state machine logic (665 lines in fr-repl) is tested in isolation, but:
- REPLICAOF returns "OK" without actually connecting to a primary
- No bytes ever flow between two FrankenRedis processes
- Cannot replicate from a real Redis primary
- A real Redis replica cannot connect to FrankenRedis as primary
- Zero cross-process replication tests exist

The PSYNC decision logic, backlog window math, and handshake FSM are correct in isolation — the gap is the actual TCP client that connects, negotiates, receives snapshots, and streams commands.

## What Success Looks Like
1. `frankenredis --replicaof host port` connects to a primary (Redis or FrankenRedis), completes PSYNC handshake, receives RDB snapshot, applies it, and enters live streaming mode
2. A second FrankenRedis instance can connect as a replica to a FrankenRedis primary
3. Writes to the primary appear on the replica within bounded latency
4. Partial resync works after brief disconnection (backlog covers the gap)
5. An integration test demonstrates this end-to-end

## Architecture
The existing fr-repl state machine and fr-server replica sync code form the skeleton. The missing pieces are:
1. Real TCP client connection in REPLICAOF handler (currently a no-op)
2. Backlog buffer that actually stores propagated commands
3. Command propagation: after each write command, serialize and push to replica write buffers
4. Snapshot streaming: encode current store as RDB and send to new replicas
5. Live command streaming: send write commands to connected replicas in real-time

## Non-Goals for V1
- Multi-master / cluster replication
- Sentinel integration
- Diskless replication (can always go through RDB file)
close_reason: All sub-tasks complete. Verified: REPLICAOF TCP client works (1v8b), RDB snapshot streaming works (de0t), backlog buffer exists via aof_records (iz1j), command propagation works (1ng7), cross-process E2E test proves FR-to-FR replication end-to-end (34b8), FEATURE_PARITY updated (8xmq). Replication status remains in_progress pending REPLICAOF runtime command fix and replica-of-replica chains.

Fix in fr-command BITCOUNT + BITFIELD OVERFLOW parsers.

title: fr-command: CLUSTER ADDSLOTSRANGE odd-args + SET-CONFIG-EPOCH missing-args reverse upstream's check ordering
description:
Upstream cluster.c::clusterCommand checks server.cluster_enabled FIRST (replying 'This instance has cluster support disabled'), then dispatches to subcommand handlers which can do further validation. The table-level commands.def arity check fires BEFORE clusterCommand though.

Two ordering issues:
1. CLUSTER ADDSLOTSRANGE 1 2 3 (argc=5): commands.def arity = -4 passes; in standalone mode upstream emits 'cluster support disabled' (the multiple-of-2 pair check is inside the subcommand handler, after the cluster gate). fr was running the pair check at the top and emitting wrong-arity instead.
2. CLUSTER SET-CONFIG-EPOCH (argc=2): commands.def arity = 3 fails table-level; upstream emits 'wrong number of arguments for cluster|set-config-epoch command'. fr emitted 'cluster support disabled' regardless.

Fix: split the ADDSLOTSRANGE/DELSLOTSRANGE arity check (only argc<4 triggers wrong-arity early; the multiple-of-2 check now runs after the cluster_enabled gate). For SET-CONFIG-EPOCH, validate argc==3 before the cluster-disabled error.

title: CRITICAL: WrongSubcommandArity emits 'subcommand' suffix; upstream Redis 7.2.4 emits 'command' — two tests fail
description:
## Summary

`cargo test --workspace` fails on main (HEAD eb6b915) with two assertions about the wording of `WrongSubcommandArity`. Our implementation special-cases `acl | slowlog | xgroup | xinfo` so the error ends with `subcommand` instead of `command`. A live probe against the vendored `legacy_redis_code/redis` binary (version 7.2.4, also reconfirmed against 7.4.8 per prior `PINNED 2026-04-22` comments in `fr-command`) shows upstream always ends the string with `command`. Our code is the divergence.

## Failing tests

- `fr-command` lib tests:
  - `lua_eval::tests::acl_admin_subcommands_reject_from_scripts_after_validation`
    - got: `ERR wrong number of arguments for 'acl|whoami' subcommand`
    - expected: `ERR wrong number of arguments for 'acl|whoami' command`
  - `tests::wrong_subcommand_arity_formats_redis_families_with_expected_wording`
    - got: `ERR wrong number of arguments for 'acl|setuser' subcommand`
    - expected: `ERR wrong number of arguments for 'acl|setuser' command`

## Live oracle probe (redis_version 7.2.4)

```
$ redis-cli ACL WHOAMI extra
ERR wrong number of arguments for 'acl|whoami' command

$ redis-cli ACL SETUSER
ERR wrong number of arguments for 'acl|setuser' command

$ redis-cli XGROUP CREATE
ERR wrong number of arguments for 'xgroup|create' command

$ redis-cli XINFO STREAM
ERR wrong number of arguments for 'xinfo|stream' command
```

Upstream source `legacy_redis_code/redis/src/server.c:3792`:
```c
*err = sdscatprintf(*err, "wrong number of arguments for '%s' command", c->cmd->fullname);
```
and `acl.c:3033`:
```c
addReplyErrorFormat(c, "wrong number of arguments for '%s' command", cmd->fullname);
```
The suffix is unconditionally `"command"`; there is no `"subcommand"` suffix anywhere.

## Where it comes from

`crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs:685-691`:
```rust
let kind = match cmd_lower.as_str() {
    "acl" | "slowlog" | "xgroup" | "xinfo" => "subcommand",
    _ => "command",
};
RespFrame::Error(format!(
    "ERR wrong number of arguments for '{}|{}' {}",
    cmd_lower, sub_lower, kind
))
```

The special-case branch fires for every subcommand of ACL/SLOWLOG/XGROUP/XINFO. This includes at least 15 `WrongSubcommandArity` call sites scattered through `fr-command` (5145..5427, 6258..10729, etc.), all of which now emit the wrong string on the wire.

Git log shows this was touched by `993bfb0 fix(command): use Redis command wording for subcommand arity` and `8f3ee5d fix(command): distinguish subcommand vs command in wrong-arity errors; FUNCTION STATS shape fixes` — the "distinguish" commit re-introduced the divergence that the earlier "use Redis wording" commit had removed.

## Suggested fix direction

Collapse the match to always emit `"command"`. The whole `kind` variable can go; the format string becomes:
```rust
format!("ERR wrong number of arguments for '{cmd_lower}|{sub_lower}' command")
```
No call-site changes needed. The existing two tests already assert the correct string.

## Labels

ci · tests · fr-command · conformance · acl · arity · upstream-divergence
close_reason: Fixed in 212bcb8 — collapsed WrongSubcommandArity suffix match to unconditional 'command' per upstream Redis 7.2.4/7.4 behavior.

title: Add pub/sub cross-client integration test via TCP
description:
# Pub/Sub Cross-Client TCP Test

## What
An integration test that demonstrates pub/sub message delivery between multiple TCP clients connected to FrankenRedis.

## Why
Pub/sub delivery is tested via internal APIs (conformance fixtures) but never through actual independent TCP connections. A subscriber on one connection must receive messages published from a completely separate connection.

## Implementation

### Test 1: Basic Pub/Sub
1. Start frankenredis on random port
2. Client A (thread 1): SUBSCRIBE channel1, wait for subscription confirmation
3. Client B (thread 2): PUBLISH channel1 "hello" → verify returns 1 (one subscriber)
4. Client A: Read next message, verify ["message", "channel1", "hello"]
5. Client A: UNSUBSCRIBE channel1

### Test 2: Pattern Subscribe
1. Client A: PSUBSCRIBE "news.*"
2. Client B: PUBLISH news.sports "goal!" → returns 1
3. Client A: Verify ["pmessage", "news.*", "news.sports", "goal!"]

### Test 3: Multiple Subscribers
1. Client A: SUBSCRIBE channel1
2. Client C: SUBSCRIBE channel1
3. Client B: PUBLISH channel1 "broadcast" → returns 2
4. Both A and C receive the message

### Test 4: Unsubscribe stops delivery
1. Client A: SUBSCRIBE channel1
2. Client B: PUBLISH channel1 "msg1" → A receives
3. Client A: UNSUBSCRIBE channel1
4. Client B: PUBLISH channel1 "msg2" → returns 0 (no subscribers)

## Logging
- Log all pub/sub operations with timestamps
- Log message received events: "[ClientA] RECEIVED: message channel1 hello"
- Log subscriber counts on PUBLISH

## Files
- crates/fr-server/tests/pubsub_e2e.rs (new)
close_reason: Added 3 pub/sub E2E tests to tcp_e2e_test.rs: tcp_pubsub_basic_cross_client_delivery (subscribe/publish/unsubscribe lifecycle), tcp_pubsub_multiple_subscribers (broadcast to 2 clients), tcp_pubsub_pattern_subscribe (PSUBSCRIBE with pmessage delivery). All pass.

title: HIGH: DUMP/RESTORE use frankenredis-private encoding — MIGRATE interop with upstream Redis impossible
description:
## Summary

`Store::dump_key` at `crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs:9433-9511` emits a payload using a **frankenredis-internal** type-byte scheme and length encoding, not the upstream RDB format. The doc comment even undersells it:

```
/// Format: [type_byte][payload][8-byte TTL-ms or 0][2-byte CRC placeholder]
```

(Actual format: 8-byte TTL, 1-byte version tag = 10, 8-byte CRC64 — so the doc comment is stale: we do write a real 8-byte CRC64 using the Redis polynomial, not a 2-byte placeholder. That part is fine.)

The deeper problem: the type bytes are flat {0=string, 1=list, 2=set, 4=hash, 5=sortedset, 15=stream} with raw-length encoding and raw-bytes payloads. Upstream RDB uses encoding-specific type tags (`RDB_TYPE_STRING`=0 with LZF or raw subtag, `RDB_TYPE_SET_INTSET`=11, `RDB_TYPE_HASH_ZIPLIST`=13, `RDB_TYPE_LIST_QUICKLIST_2`=18, `RDB_TYPE_STREAM_LISTPACKS_3`=21, etc.). Our encoding is upstream-incompatible across EVERY type.

Consequence: a client that `DUMP`s a key from FrankenRedis and `RESTORE`s into upstream Redis gets `ERR DUMP payload version or checksum are wrong` because the version byte 10 doesn't match upstream's version-field semantic (upstream gates on `rdbVersion == supported_range`, which our `10` superficially satisfies — but the payload parser fails on unknown type bytes and \"wrong\" length encoding even before the version check). Symmetrically, upstream DUMP output can't be restored into FrankenRedis — `restore_key` at lib.rs:9547 only recognizes our type bytes {0,1,2,4,5,15}.

**Impact on `MIGRATE`:** MIGRATE uses DUMP + RESTORE as its wire format. A `MIGRATE` from FrankenRedis to a real Redis node fails; the reverse too. Clustered operations are therefore fundamentally broken at the data-transfer layer, even before the cluster-admin bead (`frankenredis-jsr7`).

## Distinguishing from frankenredis-s5su

`frankenredis-s5su` covers **RDB file format** for streams specifically (type 15 inside .rdb files). This bead covers **DUMP/RESTORE wire format** for ALL types (strings, lists, sets, hashes, sorted sets, streams) — a different binary blob that flows inside a single RESP BulkString over the network, used by MIGRATE and by direct DUMP/RESTORE commands.

There is some overlap in infrastructure (both need to speak upstream's type-tag matrix), but the file-format ingestion from disk and the wire-format ingestion from MIGRATE take different code paths in upstream Redis too, and we should track them separately.

## Applying /testing-conformance-harnesses (Pattern 3: cross-impl round-trip)

- Corpus: for each upstream-supported Redis type + each upstream-supported encoding variant, capture `DUMP` output from vendored `redis-server` and store under `crates/fr-store/tests/fixtures/dump_roundtrip/`.
- Gate 1: our `restore_key` must accept every fixture without error.
- Gate 2: after RESTORE into our store, re-DUMP; feed that DUMP bytes to upstream `redis-server` and ask it to RESTORE; then query the key on upstream; canonicalize and compare to the original upstream state.
- Gate 3: the reverse direction — seed our store, DUMP, feed to upstream, read the key on upstream, canonicalize, compare to our in-memory value.

## Applying /testing-fuzzing

- `fuzz/fuzz_targets/fuzz_dump_restore.rs` exists but is self-referential (roundtrip inside FrankenRedis only). Retarget it as **differential**: `arbitrary::Arbitrary` → generate a Value, DUMP via ours, RESTORE via upstream (if present), verify roundtrip through both directions. Today this fuzzer passes because it never checks upstream; once real, it surfaces every type where our encoding diverges.

## Fix scope

- `fr-store::dump_key`: switch to upstream RDB type tags + encoding-aware serialization (reuse `fr-persist::rdb::*` helpers; they already encode full RDB records).
- `fr-store::restore_key`: dispatch on upstream type tags; reject our legacy types {1,2,4,5,15} with a one-release deprecation warning OR (better, given AGENTS.md \"no backwards-compatibility shims\") hard-remove them and regenerate any persisted .rdb fixtures in-tree.
- DUMP version tag must follow the upstream-compatible supported range (`RDB_VERSION` 11 for Redis 7.2.x, 12 for 7.4.x). Pick the one the vendored `legacy_redis_code/redis` targets and pin.

## Acceptance

- `DUMP ... → RESTORE ...` succeeds byte-identically across (FrankenRedis → FrankenRedis), (FrankenRedis → upstream), (upstream → FrankenRedis) for every upstream-observable type/encoding pair.
- `MIGRATE` from FrankenRedis to upstream moves a key successfully (observed via `GET`/`HGETALL`/etc. on the destination).
- `fuzz_dump_restore` retargeted differential-mode runs 1M iterations against the vendored oracle with zero mismatches outside documented XFAILs.

## Labels

fr-store, dump, restore, migrate, rdb-format-divergence, conformance-gap, mock-finder
close_reason: Cross-impl DUMP/RESTORE round-trip gates now cover all upstream types: STRING (lxda), STREAM (m17o), LIST/SET/SET_INTSET/SET_LISTPACK/HASH/HASH_LISTPACK/ZSET_2/ZSET_LISTPACK (v8s5). Each type exercised in both directions (ours→oracle and oracle→ours). Confirmed upstream-compatible envelope + body encoding + length/string encodings + CRC64 footer. MIGRATE wire contract gated end-to-end against vendored redis-server.

title: fr-runtime: ACL DRYRUN doesn't validate command existence/arity; CLEARSELECTORS rejected
description:
Three ACL parsing/dispatching divergences from upstream Redis 7.2:

1. ACL DRYRUN alice BADCMD: fr returns 'User alice has no permissions' but upstream returns 'ERR Command BADCMD not found' (per acl.c::aclCommand DRYRUN, command-existence check runs before the ACL gate).

2. ACL DRYRUN alice GET (no args): fr returns OK but upstream returns 'ERR wrong number of arguments for get command' (arity check runs before the ACL gate).

3. ACL SETUSER alice clearselectors: fr rejects with 'Syntax error' but upstream accepts (CLEARSELECTORS clears selector blocks). fr's ACL doesn't model selectors yet — accepting as no-op is acceptable.

title: fr-command: GEOSEARCH/GEORADIUS BYRADIUS/BYBOX non-numeric values use generic 'value is not a valid float' instead of upstream 'need numeric radius'/'width'/'height'
description:
Upstream geo.c::extractDistanceOrReply uses getDoubleFromObjectOrReply with the message 'need numeric radius', and extractBoxOrReply uses 'need numeric width' / 'need numeric height'. Negative values produce 'radius cannot be negative' / 'height or width cannot be negative' (note upstream's order is height-first).

fr-command's parse_geo_f64 emits a generic 'ERR value is not a valid float' for all callers, and the BYBOX negative-check used 'width or height cannot be negative' (reversed).

Probe vs vendored redis 7.2.4 (BYRADIUS abc m / BYBOX abc 1 m / BYBOX -1 -1 m): fr generic error vs upstream specific wording in each case.

Fix: add parse_geo_f64_with_msg(arg, msg) that lets callers thread the 'need numeric X' wording, switch GEOSEARCH and GEORADIUSBYMEMBER (synth-arg path) BYRADIUS/BYBOX call sites, and reorder BYBOX negative-check wording to upstream's 'height or width'.

title: Implement cjson.encode and cjson.decode in Lua evaluator
description:
## Problem

fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs line 1565: cjson is stubbed with a comment \"cjson stub (commonly used in Redis scripts)\". The cjson library is one of the most commonly used libraries in Redis Lua scripts for JSON serialization/deserialization.

## What Redis Provides

Redis bundles cjson (lua-cjson) which provides:
- cjson.encode(value) → JSON string
- cjson.decode(json_string) → Lua value
- cjson.encode_sparse_array(value) → JSON with sparse array handling

Common usage pattern:
  local data = cjson.decode(redis.call('GET', 'user:1'))
  data.visits = data.visits + 1
  redis.call('SET', 'user:1', cjson.encode(data))

## Implementation Plan

Since we already depend on serde_json in fr-conformance, we can add it to fr-command:

1. cjson.encode: Convert LuaValue → serde_json::Value → JSON string
   - LuaValue::Nil → JSON null
   - LuaValue::Bool → JSON bool
   - LuaValue::Number → JSON number
   - LuaValue::Str → JSON string (UTF-8)
   - LuaValue::Table → JSON object or array (based on table structure)

2. cjson.decode: Parse JSON string → serde_json::Value → LuaValue
   - JSON null → LuaValue::Nil
   - JSON bool → LuaValue::Bool
   - JSON number → LuaValue::Number
   - JSON string → LuaValue::Str
   - JSON array → LuaValue::Table (array part)
   - JSON object → LuaValue::Table (hash part)

## Edge Cases

- Empty table: encode as {} (object) or [] (array)? Redis cjson defaults to object.
- Mixed table (both array and hash keys): Redis cjson errors on this.
- Very large/deep structures: need recursion depth limit
- Non-UTF-8 strings: cjson.encode errors on these

## Files to Modify

- crates/fr-command/Cargo.toml: Add serde_json dependency
- crates/fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs: Replace stubs with real implementations
close_reason: cjson.encode/decode already fully implemented with custom JSON serializer. Fixed misleading 'stub' comment.

title: Wire CONFIG SET busy-reply-threshold (lua-time-limit) to runtime
description:
## Problem

fr-runtime/src/lib.rs line 1294 defines `const COMMAND_TIME_BUDGET_MS: u128 = 5000`. This matches Redis's busy-reply-threshold default (5000ms) but is not configurable.

## What Redis Does

CONFIG SET busy-reply-threshold <milliseconds> controls when Redis starts rejecting commands with BUSY errors during long-running scripts or operations. CONFIG SET lua-time-limit is the legacy name.

## Implementation Plan

1. Add command_time_budget_ms to ServerState (initialized to 5000)
2. Handle CONFIG SET busy-reply-threshold and lua-time-limit (both set the same value)
3. Replace the const usage with the runtime field

## Files to Modify

- crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs: Add field, CONFIG SET handler, replace const

title: [EPIC] INFO and CLIENT LIST Accuracy
description:
## Overview

Several INFO sections and CLIENT LIST output contain hardcoded or missing dynamic values. While some were fixed (hz, maxclients, executable, version), several remain.

## Remaining Issues

1. CLIENT LIST addr field: Always reports 127.0.0.1:0 instead of actual client peer address. Fixing this requires tracking peer addresses per-client in the server's session management.

2. CLIENT LIST laddr field: Partially fixed (port now dynamic) but IP is still hardcoded to 127.0.0.1.

3. INFO replication repl_backlog_size: Hardcoded to 1048576 in fr-command. Should read from runtime's actual backlog size.

4. XINFO STREAM radix-tree metrics: Hardcoded to 1/0. These require internal stream-node accounting that doesn't exist yet. Lower priority since the radix-tree is a Redis implementation detail, not a user-facing metric.

## Architecture for Client Addresses

To fix CLIENT LIST properly:
1. When accept_connections creates a new ClientConnection, store the peer addr from TcpStream::peer_addr()
2. Add a peer_addr field to ClientSession
3. In CLIENT LIST/INFO, read the stored peer addr
4. This also enables CLIENT KILL by addr filtering
close_reason: Epic acknowledged — children tasks now unblocked for implementation

title: rv89 part 1: hash field TTL storage primitives (fr-store)
description:
First slice of br-frankenredis-rv89. Add per-field TTL storage to Store without changing Value::Hash or the hash-read paths:

- New Store field: `hash_field_expires: BTreeMap<(Vec<u8>, Vec<u8>), u64>` (abs-epoch-ms).
- New Store methods:
  - `hash_field_set_abs_expiry(key, field, expires_at_ms) -> HashFieldTtlSet` with NX/XX/GT/LT semantics.
  - `hash_field_ttl_ms(key, field, now_ms) -> HashFieldTtl` returning Missing/NoTtl/Remaining(ms)/Expired.
  - `hash_field_persist(key, field) -> HashFieldPersistResult`.
  - `drop_hash_field_if_expired(key, field, now_ms)` (internal).
  - `clear_hash_field_ttls_on_hdel` hook for existing HDEL sites (wired in part 3).
- Unit tests in fr-store tests/ for: set-get-persist roundtrip; NX/XX/GT/LT; expired-field detection; key-deletion removes all field TTLs.

No fr-command commands yet (part 2). No encoding / RDB changes yet (part 4). No command-integration (part 3).
close_reason: Landed in f700569: per-field TTL storage on Store with NX/XX/GT/LT-aware setter, unit-aware HTTL/HEXPIRETIME readers, HPERSIST semantics, and HDEL/whole-key cleanup hooks. 16 unit tests pass; fr-store overall 280/0. Downstream parts 2/3/4 (c0z9, b8ut, 3jrv) consume these primitives.

title: Add keyspace hit/miss counters to Store
description:
# Add keyspace_hits and keyspace_misses counters to Store

## What
Add two new pub u64 fields to Store: stat_keyspace_hits and stat_keyspace_misses.
Increment them in the appropriate access paths.

## Where to add counters
- stat_keyspace_hits: incremented when a key lookup succeeds (get, hget, etc.)
- stat_keyspace_misses: incremented when a key lookup returns None/not-found

## Key implementation points
- Add fields to Store struct (fr-store/src/lib.rs, near line 722)
- Initialize to 0 in Store::new()
- Increment in Store::get(), Store::exists(), and other read paths that check key existence
- The tricky part: many commands call internal store methods that don't distinguish hit vs miss at the Store level. The cleanest approach is to add an internal helper that records the hit/miss after any key-existence check.
- Do NOT add counters inside every individual command — add them at the Store method level where key lookups happen.

## Why this matters
- INFO stats reports keyspace_hits:0 and keyspace_misses:0 — monitoring tools use these for cache hit ratio calculations
- Redis OBJECT FREQ uses hit frequency; while we don't implement LFU, the counters enable future LFU eviction
- Conformance: redis-benchmark and redis-stat rely on these counters

## Testing
- Unit test: call get() on existing and non-existing keys, verify counters
- Conformance: INFO stats section should show non-zero keyspace_hits after operations

## Files to modify
- crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs (Store struct + access methods)
close_reason: Completed

title: fr-persist: type-21 consumer-group payload entries_read approximation under-counts
description: Surfaced by /mock-code-finder pass. encode_consumer_group at crates/fr-persist/src/rdb_stream.rs was writing group.pending.len() for the entries_read slot in upstream STREAM_LISTPACKS_3 payloads synthesized from in-memory state. Upstream's entries_read is the cumulative count of entries the group has read (including acked ones), used by streamReplyWithRange + XINFO STREAM FULL for the lag metric. pending.len() is the unacked-only count which drops back to zero on every XACK. Using the latter inflates the upstream-side lag every time a consumer acks anything.
close_reason: Shipped in 433c413: encode_consumer_group at crates/fr-persist/src/rdb_stream.rs:147 now emits the upstream SCG_INVALID_ENTRIES_READ sentinel (-1 cast to u64, encoded as 0x81 + 8 x 0xFF) for the entries_read slot when fr-persist synthesizes the type-21 payload from in-memory state. Upstream's loadrdb path then falls back to streamEstimateDistanceFromFirstEverEntry for lag computation rather than trusting the under-counted pending.len() approximation. Locked in by new unit test encode_consumer_group_writes_scg_invalid_entries_read_sentinel which byte-scans the encoded payload for the 9-byte sentinel sequence and explicitly asserts the entries_read slot byte is 0x81 (NOT 0x02 = old buggy pending.len()=2). cargo test -p fr-persist 138/138 green.

description: FR-P2C-005 Stage D3 upstream parity: add fr-persist RDB preamble decode cursor accounting so an AOF file with a REDIS RDB preamble can hand off exactly to the RESP AOF tail without cursor drift. Keep whole-RDB decode strict on trailing bytes and cover U002/I02 with unit tests.

title: fr-command Lua: redis.error_reply doesn't prepend ERR error code when missing
description:
Lua's `redis.error_reply("err")` should emit the wire reply '-ERR err\r\n', producing 'ERR err' as displayed by redis-cli. fr instead returns just 'err' (no error code prefix) because crates/fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs:2463 stores the raw arg verbatim in the {err=...} table.

Upstream script_lua.c::luaRedisErrorReplyCommand prepends '-' when missing, then luaPushErrorBuff parses leading '-CODE [msg]' (split by first space) — if the body has no space, code defaults to 'ERR' and the rest is the message. The table is built as {err = '<CODE> <msg>'}.

Acceptance:
  EVAL 'return redis.error_reply("err")' 0 → '-ERR err\r\n' on the wire (redis-cli prints 'ERR err')
  EVAL 'return redis.error_reply("CUSTOM hi")' 0 → '-ERR CUSTOM hi\r\n' (since no leading '-')
  EVAL 'return redis.error_reply("-CUSTOM hi")' 0 → '-CUSTOM hi\r\n' (leading '-' parsed, code=CUSTOM, msg=hi)
  EVAL 'return redis.error_reply("-NOMSG")' 0 → '-ERR NOMSG\r\n' (no space after '-CODE' → fallback to default ERR)

Caught by ad-hoc differential probe of fr (port 16700) vs vendored Redis 7.2.4 (port 16701): EVAL 'return redis.error_reply("err")' 0. fr: 'err'. redis: 'ERR err'.

title: Wire real counters into INFO stats section
description:
# Wire real counters into INFO stats section

## What
Replace hardcoded zeros in fr-command's info() stats section with real Store counter values. After the foundation counter beads land, these fields become simple reads.

## Fields to fix (fr-command/src/lib.rs, info() function, stats section ~line 8290):
1. expired_keys → store.stat_expired_keys
2. expired_stale_perc → store.stat_expired_stale_perc (or compute from cycle stats)
3. expired_time_cap_reached_count → 0 is acceptable (cap not implemented)
4. expire_cycle_cpu_milliseconds → store.stat_expire_cycle_cpu_milliseconds
5. evicted_keys → store.stat_evicted_keys
6. total_keys_expired → store.stat_expired_keys (same counter)
7. total_keys_evicted → store.stat_evicted_keys (same counter)
8. keyspace_hits → store.stat_keyspace_hits
9. keyspace_misses → store.stat_keyspace_misses

## Fields in clients section (~line 8198):
10. blocked_clients → store.stat_blocked_clients (populated by server)
11. tracking_clients → 0 (acceptable until CLIENT TRACKING is fully wired)

## Fields in memory section (~line 8218):
12. maxmemory → store.maxmemory_bytes_live (populated by runtime)

## Fields in CPU section (~line 8372):
These require platform-specific code (reading /proc/self/stat on Linux). Lower priority — acceptable to leave as 0 with a comment explaining why.

## Depends on
- frankenredis-xf0u (keyspace hit/miss counters)
- frankenredis-jg50 (expired/evicted counters)
- frankenredis-yf1j (runtime context fields)

## Testing

- Unit test: set + get key, verify keyspace_hits incremented in INFO output

title: [HIGH] XADD auto-ID + broad stream divergences — fixture clock vs upstream wall clock
description: Surfaced by live_redis_core_stream_matches_runtime: 48/218 divergences; the majority stem from XADD auto-generated IDs using the fixture now_ms (e.g. '1000-0') while upstream redis-server stamps entries with its wall-clock ms (e.g. '1777004357777-0'). Dependent commands (XRANGE, XREAD, XLEN after deletion, XPENDING) then cascade mismatches because they reference those IDs. Fix options: (a) add stream-specific canonicalization that normalizes '*' auto-IDs in the expected/actual frames before compare; (b) regenerate the fixture after a harness upgrade that stubs now_ms on the upstream side via EVAL or CLIENT NO-EVICT; (c) split the stream fixture into 'deterministic-id' (explicit ms-seq) vs 'auto-id' suites and only run the former under live oracle. Also: xadd_explicit_higher_id — ours accepts a new entry where upstream rejects with 'ERR The ID specified in XADD is equal or smaller than the target stream top item', independent of the clock issue.
close_reason: Already resolved: live_redis_core_stream_matches_runtime passes 100% under FR_CONFORMANCE_LIVE_ORACLE_STRICT=1 (fixture-clock canonicalizer on upstream stream IDs landed previously).

title: fr-command: XINFO STREAM key-lookup ordering + arg-validation wording divergences
description:
Three divergences in XINFO STREAM vs vendored redis 7.2.4 t_stream.c::xinfoCommand:

1. Key-lookup ordering: upstream looks up the key BEFORE dispatching to the STREAM/FULL branch, so 'XINFO STREAM nokey FULL extra' replies 'no such key'. fr validated args first, leaking 'wrong number of arguments' for missing keys.

2. Arg-validation wording: when the key exists but the FULL [COUNT] tail is malformed, upstream's xinfoReplyWithStreamInfo calls addReplySubcommandSyntaxError producing 'unknown subcommand or wrong number of arguments for STREAM. Try XINFO HELP.'. fr emitted 'wrong number of arguments for xinfo|stream command'.

3. Negative COUNT: upstream silently treats a negative COUNT as the default 10 (count<0 → count=10). fr returned an InvalidInteger error.

Fix:
- Move xinfo_stream() lookup ahead of full-mode arg parsing so missing key short-circuits.
- Replace WrongSubcommandArity emissions in the STREAM branch with a closure that builds the addReplySubcommandSyntaxError-style wording.
- Default negative COUNT to 10.

title: Implement lexical scoping / upvalue capture in Lua evaluator
description:
## Problem

fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs line 2274: When calling a LuaValue::Function, a completely fresh environment is created (Env::new()). This means closures cannot access variables from their defining scope.

Example that breaks:
  local x = 10
  local f = function() return x end
  return f()  -- returns nil, should return 10

This is a FUNDAMENTAL semantic bug that silently breaks any Lua code using closures, callbacks, or higher-order functions.

## How Real Lua Does It

Lua 5.1 uses upvalue capture: when a function is created, it captures references to local variables in enclosing scopes. These captured variables (upvalues) travel with the function closure.

## Implementation Options

### Option A: Environment Chain (simpler)
When creating a LuaValue::Function, snapshot the current environment as a parent chain. When the function is called, create a new env with the captured parent as the lookup fallback.

Data model change:
- LuaValue::Function currently stores (name, body_statements)
- Change to store (name, body_statements, captured_env: Option<Env>)
- In Env::get(), if local lookup fails, walk the captured_env chain

### Option B: Explicit Upvalue Tracking (more correct)
Track which variables are referenced by the function body. Only capture those specific bindings. More memory-efficient but harder to implement.

## Recommendation

Option A is much simpler and sufficient for Redis Lua scripts (which are typically not deeply nested). The performance cost is minimal since Env is just a HashMap.

## Files to Modify

- crates/fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs: LuaValue::Function variant, Env struct, call_function, function definition handling

## Testing

- Test: local variable access from closure
- Test: nested closures (closure inside closure)
- Test: closure over loop variable
- Test: multiple closures sharing same upvalue
- Test: redis.call inside a closure
close_reason: Implemented upvalue capture via captured_env on LuaFunc. Functions now snapshot the lexical scope at definition time and restore it on call, enabling closures.

title: Add runtime-populated context fields to Store for INFO accuracy
description:
# Add runtime context fields to Store for cross-layer INFO reporting

## What
Add fields to Store that the runtime/server populates each tick so that fr-command's INFO handler can report accurate values for fields that originate in the runtime layer:
- pub stat_blocked_clients: u64 (number of currently blocked clients)
- pub stat_tracking_clients: u64 (number of tracking clients)
- pub maxmemory_bytes_live: usize (runtime's current maxmemory setting)

## Why a Store field instead of duplicating logic
The INFO command is dispatched through fr-command's dispatch_argv, which only has access to Store. The runtime has the real values but can't easily pass them through dispatch_argv's signature. The cleanest solution is for the server event loop to update these Store fields before each command batch, so INFO reads accurate values.

## Where to populate
- In fr-server/src/main.rs: before processing each client's commands, update store.stat_blocked_clients = blocked_tokens.len()
- In fr-runtime: when maxmemory changes, update store.maxmemory_bytes_live
- tracking_clients can stay 0 until CLIENT TRACKING is fully wired

## Key implementation points
- Add fields to Store struct with pub visibility
- Initialize to 0 in Store::new()
- Server event loop updates them each tick (cheap — just integer assignments)
- INFO handler in fr-command reads them instead of hardcoding 0

## Why this matters
- blocked_clients:0 is wrong when clients are blocked on BLPOP etc.
- maxmemory:0 in INFO is wrong when runtime has set maxmemory
- Monitoring tools use these for capacity planning

## Files to modify
- crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs (Store struct)
- crates/fr-server/src/main.rs (populate fields each tick)
- crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs (populate maxmemory_bytes_live on CONFIG SET)
close_reason: Completed

title: fr-command: MEMORY DOCTOR returns short canned message instead of upstream's verbose empty-instance text
description:
MEMORY DOCTOR returns 'Sam, I have no memory problems' (one line) for any state. Upstream Redis 7.2 (object.c::getMemoryDoctorReport) has three branches:
  (a) when total_allocated < 5 MB ('empty' branch) → emits the verbose 'Hi Sam, this instance is empty or is using very little memory, my issues detector can't be used in these conditions. Please, leave for your mission on Earth and fill it with some data. The new Sam and I will be back to our programming as soon as I finished rebooting.\n'
  (b) when num_reports == 0 (no issues, not empty) → 'Hi Sam, I can\'t find any memory issue in your instance. I can only account for what occurs on this base.\n'
  (c) when there are issues → a multi-bullet report with sections.

Acceptance: an idle fr-server with no keys returns the (a) verbose empty-instance message verbatim (matching vendored redis-server 7.2.4 byte-for-byte). When dbsize > 0 (or some heuristic for non-empty) but no detected issues, returns the (b) 'I can\'t find any memory issue' line. The (c) verbose-issues path can stay as a follow-up since fr does not yet track allocator/peak/RSS metrics with enough fidelity to surface them.

Reference: ran 'MEMORY DOCTOR' against fr (port 16700) vs vendored redis (port 16701) on a fresh empty instance. fr: 'Sam, I have no memory problems'. redis: 'Hi Sam, this instance is empty or is using very little memory...'. Caught by ad-hoc differential probe.

Fix touches fr-command/src/lib.rs around the MEMORY DOCTOR branch (~line 13487). The conformance fixture core_server.json has 2 cases (memory_doctor + memory_doctor_duplicate_block) pinning the old short message — those need updating.

title: Precompute ACL category->command mapping in OnceLock
description:
# Precompute ACL Category→Command Mapping in OnceLock

## What

command_categories() in crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs:62 and
commands_in_acl_category() in crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs:8924 recompute
ACL category→command mappings from scratch on every call. Each call iterates
21 ACL categories × the entire COMMAND_TABLE (~200 commands) and does
split_whitespace().collect::<Vec<&str>>() on each command's flags string —
~4,200 Vec allocations per invocation.

After the April 9 short-circuit (frankenredis-zjii), the DEFAULT user (no
category rules) never hits this code path. But any user with ACL category
rules (e.g., +@read -@dangerous) still pays the full cost on every command.

## Why This Matters

Production Redis deployments commonly use ACL categories for least-privilege
security. A web app might configure: user webuser +@read +@write -@dangerous.
That user has non-empty allowed_categories, so the short-circuit doesn't apply,
and every command pays ~4,200 allocations for category resolution.

## Design

Use OnceLock<HashMap<&'static str, Vec<&'static str>>> to build the mapping
once at startup:

```rust
static ACL_CATEGORY_MAP: OnceLock<HashMap<&'static str, Vec<&'static str>>> = OnceLock::new();

fn commands_in_acl_category_cached(category: &str) -> &'static [&'static str] {
    let map = ACL_CATEGORY_MAP.get_or_init(|| {
        let mut m = HashMap::new();
        for &cat in ACL_CATEGORIES {
            m.insert(cat, commands_in_acl_category_uncached(cat));
        }
        m
    });
    map.get(category).map(|v| v.as_slice()).unwrap_or(&[])
}
```

Also precompute the reverse mapping (command→categories) so
command_categories() is a single HashMap lookup:

```rust
static COMMAND_CATEGORY_MAP: OnceLock<HashMap<&'static str, Vec<&'static str>>> = OnceLock::new();
```

## Acceptance Criteria

1. commands_in_acl_category uses OnceLock-cached mapping
2. command_categories uses OnceLock-cached reverse mapping
3. Zero per-command allocations for ACL category resolution
4. All ACL conformance tests pass
5. Users with non-trivial ACL rules see no throughput regression vs default user
6. Memory overhead is bounded (21 categories × 200 commands = ~4200 pointers = ~33KB)
close_reason: Cached ACL category mappings + command reverse map

title: Investigate severe throughput gap vs Redis baselines
description:
# Severe Performance Gap Against Redis Baseline

## What
Investigate and remediate the initial benchmark evidence showing FrankenRedis is drastically slower than legacy Redis across the basic workload set.

## Evidence
From `baselines/` captured on 2026-04-07:

- `SET`: FrankenRedis `1240.93 ops/sec` vs Redis `94402.47 ops/sec` (`1.31%` of Redis throughput)
- `GET`: FrankenRedis `1204.65 ops/sec` vs Redis `91142.35 ops/sec` (`1.32%` of Redis throughput)
- `MIXED`: FrankenRedis `1159.23 ops/sec` vs Redis `96834.08 ops/sec` (`1.20%` of Redis throughput)
- `INCR`: FrankenRedis `1176.59 ops/sec` vs Redis `95183.76 ops/sec` (`1.24%` of Redis throughput)
- `PIPELINE16`: FrankenRedis `1221.37 ops/sec` vs Redis `860900.42 ops/sec` (`0.14%` of Redis throughput)

Latency evidence is equally severe:
- `SET` p99: `58,847us` vs Redis `1,022us`
- `GET` p99: `51,647us` vs Redis `1,038us`
- `PIPELINE16` p50/p99: `693,759us / 857,087us` vs Redis `759us / 1,817us`

## Why This Matters
The `frankenredis-gess` bead explicitly required at least `50%` of legacy Redis throughput for the basic SET/GET baselines. The recorded baselines are far below that threshold, and pipeline depth does not improve throughput at all, which strongly suggests a structural bottleneck in the server/event-loop/runtime path.

## Investigation Targets
1. Profile FrankenRedis under the `set`, `get`, and `pipeline16` workloads.
2. Determine whether the dominant bottleneck is in:
   - `fr-server` event loop / socket IO
   - `fr-runtime::execute_frame` dispatch path
   - RESP parsing / encoding overhead
   - store keyspace representation / DB namespacing
   - per-command bookkeeping (`INFO` stats, slowlog, latency tracker, evidence logging)
3. Produce a benchmark delta report after the first optimization lever.
4. Preserve behavior with conformance evidence before and after each change.

## Exit Criteria
- Root-cause profile captured and checked in under `artifacts/benchmark/` or `artifacts/optimization/`
- First targeted optimization landed with before/after benchmark delta
- Follow-up bead(s) created for remaining bottlenecks if the full gap is not closed in one pass

HGETALL order is technically unspecified in Redis, but the fixture was captured from upstream. Either canonicalize in the harness (sort pairs by field) or switch our hash backend to insertion-order for parity with small-hash listpack encoding.
